notes: Each eval provides an already-scraped history.json so we test the analysis/report stage (the judgment-heavy part). The Playwright scraping stage is verified manually since it needs real logged-in YouTube data. Ground truth for assertions lives in fixtures/_truth.json.

evals.name: heavy-dev-viewer
evals.prompt: I already pulled my YouTube watch history into history.json in this folder. Analyze it and tell me what kind of YouTube viewer I am — especially how much developer/technical stuff I watch and what style it is. Write it to stats-report.md.
evals.expected_output: stats-report.md identifying a heavy developer viewer (~80% dev), dominated by tutorials/personality/talks/podcasts, with topic + channel + estimated watch-time breakdowns and the watch-time-is-an-estimate caveat.

assertions.text: Identifies the developer/technical share as roughly 80% (accept 70-90%)
Reports a video-style breakdown including at least tutorial and podcast/long-form styles
Lists top channels (e.g. names real channels like Fireship, ThePrimeagen, Tsoding)
Gives watch-time figures and explicitly labels them as estimates based on video length, not actual watch time
Includes a 'what kind of viewer are you' style summary that is specific, not generic

evals.name: casual-non-dev-viewer
evals.prompt: can you look at my youtube history (it's in history.json here) and break down what i watch? curious if i actually watch much techy/dev content or if it's mostly other stuff. save the writeup as stats-report.md
evals.expected_output: stats-report.md identifying a mostly non-developer viewer (~10% dev), dominated by music/entertainment/personality/podcast, correctly NOT overstating the dev share.

assertions.text: Identifies the developer/technical share as low, roughly 10% (accept 5-20%)
Does NOT mislabel music/vlog/entertainment videos as developer content
Surfaces the dominant non-dev topics/styles (music, entertainment, personality, podcast)
Watch-time figures are present and labelled as estimates

evals.name: shorts-heavy-viewer
evals.prompt: Analyze my last 30 days of YouTube from history.json in this directory. I feel like I doomscroll Shorts way too much — is that true? Put the report in stats-report.md.
evals.expected_output: stats-report.md that flags Shorts dominate video COUNT (~40 of 43) but contribute little estimated TIME vs the few long-form videos, separating counts from time so the picture isn't misleading.

assertions.text: Reports that Shorts make up the large majority of videos by count (~40 of 43, accept 'most'/'90%+')
Explicitly distinguishes video count from estimated watch time, noting Shorts inflate counts but add little time relative to the long-form videos
Does not present estimated watch time as exact/actual watched seconds
Gives a direct answer to the user's 'do I doomscroll Shorts' question
